turns.content: I'm trying to implement a thread pool using the deque crate for work stealing but I get this error: 'cannot find value `crossbeam` in module' when compiling my worker thread initialization code. What went wrong?

It looks like your Cargo.toml is missing the correct dependency declaration for crossbeam-deque. You need to add 'crossbeam = "0.8"' under dependencies, not just reference the crate name in your code without linking it first.
Okay I added that but now my worker threads are stuck and never get work assigned to them. The deque seems empty even though tasks are being created.

The issue is likely that your worker thread isn't actually grabbing items from the crossbeam::deque. In a proper implementation, each worker should spin or poll the queue while holding a mutex lock to ensure safe access, but if you're not polling correctly or the lock scope is wrong, threads will just sit idle.
I thought I was using the standard iterator pattern on the deque but that doesn't work for concurrent access. How should I actually poll this data structure safely?

You cannot iterate over a crossbeam::deque in a multi-threaded context without synchronization because it's not inherently thread-safe like std::collections::VecDeque. You must hold a lock acquired via the appropriate mutex type to safely peek or pop from the queue while your worker thread is running.
So I need a Mutex wrapping my crossbeam-deque instance and then manually poll it inside the worker loop before releasing the lock?

Exactly, that's the core pattern. Your worker thread needs to acquire the lock, check if there are items using methods like pop or try_pop, execute the work, then release the lock so other workers can also access the shared crossbeam::deque.

Hey, I'm stuck on my thread pool implementation using the deque crate and crossbeam-deque. I tried to build a simple worker/stealer pattern but keep hitting deadlocks because my stealer isn't actually stealing work efficiently. Here's what I have so far: let mut queue = crossbeam::sync::Deque::new(); let workers = vec![std::thread::spawn(move || { loop { if !queue.try_pop_front().is_none() { process_work(queue.pop_back()) } else { break } })}; ...

The issue you're running into is a classic beginner mistake when building work-stealing queues: assuming that simply checking the front of the queue and then trying to pop from the back will allow other threads to steal. Your current logic only lets one thread consume items because you're not leveraging the crossbeam::sync::Deque's ability to let multiple workers compete for the same data structure. The real problem is that your stealer isn't actually stealing; it's just peeking. To fix this, you need to implement a proper work-stealing algorithm where each worker has its own deque or uses a global one with a specific locking strategy. In Rust, the crossbeam::sync::Deque type supports atomic operations via TryPopFront and PopBack which are exactly what you need for concurrent access without explicit locks. The key insight is that when building a thread pool, you shouldn't just check if there's work; instead, you should attempt to steal from other workers' queues or use a global queue where multiple threads can safely pop items using the atomic operations provided by crossbeam-deque.

Ah okay so I was thinking of locking manually around my peek and pop calls which defeats the whole point of using crossbeam. Can you explain why the TryPopFront approach actually works better than a manual lock? And what happens if I just use PopBack for all threads without any stealing logic?

You're right that manual locks defeat the purpose here. The reason TryPopFront works better is because it uses atomic compare-and-swap operations internally within crossbeam::sync::Deque, which allows multiple threads to safely inspect and remove items simultaneously without race conditions or deadlocks. When you use PopBack for all threads without stealing logic, you're essentially creating a producer-consumer bottleneck where only the thread that produced work can consume it unless you explicitly implement the stealing pattern. The crossbeam-deque crate is designed specifically to solve this by providing a data structure where workers can both produce and steal work efficiently. Your stealer needs to actively attempt to pop items from other threads' queues or use a global queue with multiple producers, not just rely on sequential access.

That makes sense now. So the real fix is implementing actual stealing logic using crossbeam's atomic operations rather than manual synchronization primitives? And I should probably use std::thread::spawn for my workers instead of some other approach?

Exactly, you should implement active stealing logic using crossbeam's atomic operations like TryPopFront and PopBack to allow multiple threads to safely access the shared queue without manual synchronization. You don't need std::thread::spawn specifically; in fact, using a thread pool abstraction or a framework that manages thread lifecycle is often better for production code. The key takeaway is that your current implementation fails because it doesn't leverage the atomic operations provided by crossbeam-deque to enable concurrent access patterns. Once you switch from manual locking to using TryPopFront and PopBack correctly with actual stealing logic, your work-stealing queue should function properly without deadlocks or bottlenecks.

I'm trying to build a thread pool in Rust but my current approach with std::sync::mpsc isn't scaling well. I thought using the deque crate would help, but it seems like crossbeam-deque is actually better for work stealing.

You're absolutely right about crossbeam-deque being superior to std::sync::mpsc for this use case. The deque crate provides a more efficient implementation of the work-stealing queue pattern that's designed specifically for parallel execution, whereas mpsc is just a basic channel and doesn't support the stealing mechanism you need.
That makes sense. So with crossbeam-deque I can have multiple workers pull from the same queue? But how exactly do I implement the stealer logic since that's not part of the crate itself?

Exactly, you need to manually manage the worker and stealer threads using crossbeam-deque. The crate only provides the underlying data structure, so you'll need to write code where each worker thread gets a handle to the deque and periodically checks if it has work, while the stealer runs in its own thread that pulls items from any worker's queue when idle.
I'm confused about the synchronization part. If I use atomic bools to track which threads are stealing, will that prevent deadlocks?

Good catch on potential deadlock risks. You should avoid using simple atomic booleans for coordination because they can cause contention issues in high-throughput scenarios. Instead, consider using a lock-free approach with crossbeam's atomics or implementing proper synchronization primitives that don't introduce unnecessary blocking.
What about the difference between using std::sync::Mutex and some other locking mechanism in this context?

In a work-stealing scenario, you generally want to minimize synchronization overhead. While std::sync::Mutex works for simple cases, it can become a bottleneck when many threads are trying to access shared state simultaneously. For crossbeam-deque based pools, the deque itself is lock-free so you should avoid adding extra mutexes unless absolutely necessary.
So I'm still confused about how exactly the stealer interacts with the workers without causing contention. Can we clarify that part?

The key insight is that the stealer only operates when a worker thread explicitly signals it's idle or needs help. You can use crossbeam's thread-local storage to track whether each worker has work pending, and only the stealer accesses the shared deque structure itself. This way you avoid contention because workers don't directly access the main queue.
